l0.mandiPriceFrequency: daily, by crop x market, free

bananaGlobalRank.note: India is the world's #1 banana producer

bananaFruitEconomyShare.note: India's #2 fruit by land and value (mango #1 at 29% of fruit output value)

bananaYearRoundTN.note: Plantable nearly any time of year in Tamil Nadu, except peak summer -- unlike seasonal fruits, so supply-smoothing (staggered planting) is agronomically available here specifically

theniClusterStats.note: Stable at 17,000 Ha, production growing via high-density planting and drip fertigation; Kerala absorbs nearly all of the cluster's surplus

clusterLossComparison.note: TN clusters (incl. Theni) hold the lowest total post-harvest loss of any surveyed banana cluster in India

giHeadroomTheni.note: Theni holds two GI tags with zero brand recall -- the headroom the Jalgaon Banana GI has already proven (+50%/kg premium)

bananaFarmerShare.note: Lowest of the three fruits studied (grapes 35%, mango 42-43%)

bananaStageContent.note: Exact stage-wise %-split is image-only (Chart 29, p.30) — not extractable. Farmer share is the verified anchor; stage content is descriptive, not a fabricated split.

ripeningCharge.note: MIDH-assisted units, Theni; high electricity load

bananaElasticity.note: long-run ARDL: +1% availability-usage ratio -> -0.21% banana CPI

mangoFarmerShare.note: Highest of the three fruits, WITH a pre-harvest contractor (PHC) layer — the contractor is a bundle, not a toll

bananaCorridorIllustrative.conflictNote: This corridor implies ~52% farmer share (13/25), against RBI's verified 30.8% for the same corridor description — different year/source, or a NotebookLM blend across sources. Treated as an illustrative build-up, not the RBI decomposition, until reconciled to a primary page.

bananaChainUplift.note: BVC1 (traditional multi-layer chain) vs BVC2 (modern wholesale agency with farmer co-ownership, bypasses retail-level commission agents) — South India case study; the citable value-prop number

bvcPrecedent.note: BVC1 = traditional multi-layer chain (runs on sociocultural trust: farmers rarely bargain, accept payment delays, trust buyers to absorb crash losses). BVC2 = farmer-co-owned modern wholesale agency -- aggregates, controls ripening, sells direct to supermarkets/retailers/city wholesalers. The precedent sits inside our own cluster.

corridorLengthComparison.note: Distance is a structural determinant of farmer share -- proximity is a Theni-cluster advantage, not just a chain-design advantage.

contractorAdvanceTerms.pricingNote: zero-bargaining fixed pricing; buyer absorbs 100% of crash losses
contractorAdvanceTerms.costNote: the advance costs the farmer price, not interest -- the moat is trust and convenience, not rate

commissionRateByRegion.note: TN-specific rate (Pauline & Ajjan, TNAU 2014); commission agents handle 85-90% of farmer volume nationwide regardless of rate

gradeSpreadCapture.note: Grading happens post-sale, in the contractor's hands -- the farmer sells at a flat price before grading and never captures the A/B/C spread

retailResidualDerived.note: Derived, not directly reported: Theni commission-agent farmgate (Rs 9-10/kg) divided by the BVC1 traditional-chain farmer share (52%) implies a local retail price of roughly Rs 17-19/kg. Shown as a labeled estimate, not a verified retail quote.

theniGroups: Tamil Nadu Banana Producers Federation (60 members)

ripeningChamberCapex.note: Credit-linked, back-ended subsidy. Ripening chamber is an add-on component under the Integrated Post-Harvest Management (IPHM) project cap, not a standalone scheme.

ripeningChamberOpexGap.knownCostProxy: the Rs 2/kg usage charge (ripeningCharge, above) is the only cost figure found anywhere
ripeningChamberOpexGap.bindingConstraint: electricity, flagged explicitly as 'high' in the primary source table
ripeningChamberOpexGap.note: No Rs/kWh or Rs/kg-processed opex breakdown exists in any primary or secondary source found. This is a genuine research gap, not a rounding -- any per-kg opex or margin figure for FPO-operated ripening must be labeled illustrative/unsourced until this closes.

bananaPseudostemExtended.note: Extends bananaPseudostem (above) with the B-grade fiber band and the biogas-feedstock revenue stream; both roll into the same +15.5-17% farm profitability uplift figure already logged.

platformFeeBenchmarks.note: Ninjacart figures are a blended trading-book gross margin, not a published fee schedule -- loosest available comparable, not a benchmark for our fee. A circulating 'ONDC charges 2-5% vs 15-25% on Amazon/Flipkart-type platforms' claim was checked and REJECTED: its sole source is an uncited agritech blog with no methodology or government citation. Do not cite the 2-5% figure as fact anywhere.

platformFeeWorkingAssumption.note: Our own working assumption for the platform/FPO per-kg linkage fee. No external benchmark closes it -- Ninjacart's 12-15% gross margin is the loosest available comparable (a trading-book margin, not a fee schedule) and the circulating 'ONDC 2-5%' figure is rejected as unsourced. Present as an assumption to be tested, never as a benchmarked or validated number.

backhaulEconomics.note: All-India generic road-freight benchmarks, not banana- or Theni-specific. NITI Aayog's primary 'Fast Tracking Freight in India' PDF returned HTTP 403 and could not be opened; these numbers are reported secondhand via a logistics-industry blog citing CRISIL/Logistics Sector Skill Council. No Milkymist (TN dairy) or Kashmir-apple sector-specific backhaul benchmark was found despite searching -- flag any Theni-specific backhaul revenue line as modelled off a generic proxy, not a sector precedent.

fpoCreditSubstitutes.note: Central Sector FPO scheme provisions. Chinnamanur FPC (the BVC2 precedent village, inside Theni) extends credit and extension services to 100% of its active members -- a real-world data point for what a functioning member-credit mechanism looks like, though not a description of OUR proposed mechanism.

own.basis: the core asset -- coordination logic lives here
the e-Choupal law: asset-light coordination over ownership

rentOrAccessYear0to2.basis: allocated by order, not owned; 5 govt-MIDH units already exist in Theni (895 MT) plus household units
rents/partners; hub-and-spoke uses existing haulage (Tata Ace / Eicher class)
never off own balance sheet -- partner rails only (KCC/pledge/anchor); FPO working capital is structurally too thin (median <Rs 3 lakh) to originate credit

neverOwned.basis: farmers retain ownership; the FPO is a coordination layer over independent smallholdings (146M holdings nationally, 86% <2 ha)

maybeYear3Plus.basis: now costable: capex Rs 1.00 lakh/MT, MIDH/NHB subsidy 35% general / 50% hilly-NE -- see ripeningChamberCapex

assetOwnershipBoundary.note: This is the FPO's proposed own-vs-rent operating posture, not an externally sourced fact -- it is our design decision, built on top of sourced figures (chamber capex/subsidy, working-capital ceiling, existing Theni infrastructure) that are individually cited elsewhere in this file.

functions.before: Contractor guesses/reacts to market; farmer grows blind
functions.platform: Retailer order book (volume, grade, delivery cadence, forecast) drives planting schedule before harvest

functions.before: Post-sale, in contractor's hands -- farmer never captures the A/B/C spread (Rs 10/8/5, a 2x gap)
functions.platform: Farm-gate, pre-sale, with a digital lot ID (QR) attached -- farmer captures part of the grade spread

functions.before: Contractor's interest-free advance, cost extracted through price (zero-bargaining, up to 3-week payment delays, buyer absorbs crash losses)
functions.platform: Day-0 digital payment on partner rails only (KCC/pledge/anchor finance) -- never the FPO's own book

functions.before: Rs 2/kg toll, allocation-blind, dwell-time and loss unmanaged
functions.platform: Allocated by order -- matched to grade x buyer delivery timeline, same toll, less loss
Hub-and-spoke: Theni collection spokes -> ripening-chamber hubs -> retail; return legs loaded with Karnataka-cluster goods (coffee etc.) for max truck uptime

functions.before: None -- RBI explicitly states banana has no GrapeNet/MangoNet equivalent
functions.platform: QR lot ID: farmer, village, harvest date, grade, ripening status -- retailers subscribe to recurring supply against a spec

sixFunctionReroute.note: The 'before' column cells cite already-sourced figures (Rs 2/kg ripening toll, 10% TN commission, A/B/C grade spread, RBI traceability-gap finding). The 'platform' column is our proposed operating model -- a re-routing of the same functions, not a sourced fact. Present the before column as fact, the platform column as proposed design.

stages.description: Use existing govt-MIDH ripening chambers (5 units, 895 MT, Theni) via order-allocated scheduling. No capex, no lease, no opex risk.
Priority/contracted access to existing chamber capacity at the same Rs 2/kg market toll, secured via volume commitment -- still no ownership.
FPO leases dedicated chamber capacity once volume and grade-timing predictability justify it.
FPO owns chamber capacity outright (capex Rs 1.00 lakh/MT, 35-50% subsidized) once scale and unit economics are proven.

stageGates.condition: Proven order-book volume through existing capacity over multiple seasons

Known electricity cost per kg processed -- CURRENTLY UNSOURCED. No Rs/kWh or Rs/kg opex figure exists anywhere (see ripeningChamberOpexGap). This gate cannot be honestly claimed as passable until that number exists.

Demonstrated positive lease-vs-own economics across multiple seasons, i.e. leased throughput and cost data showing ownership beats leasing at the FPO's actual volume -- not yet modeled, no reference case exists.

ripeningOwnershipStages.note: This entire staged framework (access -> control -> lease -> own) is a PROPOSED operating-model construct, not sourced anywhere in the vault research. It borrows the Sahyadri precedent's general pattern (assets follow order-book proof, not the other way round -- Precedents.md) but the four named stages and their gate conditions are our own design, not independently validated against any real FPO's actual ripening-asset acquisition path. Present explicitly as 'how we propose to stage it,' never as an established model.

chamberFeeSetting.description: Proposed mechanism: chamber usage fee set at or below the prevailing Rs 2/kg market toll (ripeningCharge), reviewed seasonally by an FPO board committee, with any FPO-operated-chamber margin capped and disclosed to members.
chamberFeeSetting.note: Proposed governance mechanism, not yet validated against a real FPO's bylaws. No existing FPO fee-setting bylaw was found in the research to model this on directly.

memberPatronageDistribution.description: Proposed: platform-fee surplus (after ops cost) distributed to members pro-rata to volume supplied through the platform, on a patronage-dividend model, disclosed at each settlement cycle.
memberPatronageDistribution.note: Proposed governance mechanism, not yet validated against a real FPO's bylaws. This is explicitly the 'FPO capture mechanism' flagged as an open design question in the Handoff Spec §5/§7 -- asserted as intent, not proven or specified in enforceable bylaw language.

riskReserve.description: Proposed: a small percentage of platform-fee revenue held back into a reserve fund to absorb price-crash/quality-dispute shocks, so the FPO does not have to pass 100% of downside back onto members mid-season the way informal contractors currently absorb (or don't) crash losses.
riskReserve.note: Proposed governance mechanism, not yet validated against a real FPO's bylaws. No sourced reserve-ratio precedent exists in the research; any specific percentage shown on the site must be labeled illustrative.

fpoCaptureMechanismOpenQuestion.description: The e-Choupal precedent proves coordination-over-ownership works, but ITC (the buyer) captured the uplift, not farmers. Our stated delta is that because the FPO is farmer-owned, uplift should accrue to members -- but there is no citable mechanism (bylaws, profit-distribution formula, membership economics) establishing HOW capture is guaranteed rather than merely intended.
fpoCaptureMechanismOpenQuestion.note: This is the single most important honesty flag in the governance section. State it as an explicitly open question if asked, not as solved.

scalingRoadmapSequencing.principle: Asset-light is the year-0-2 posture. The FPO's own-vs-rent line moves right as volume proves out; it does not start there.
scalingRoadmapSequencing.precedent: Sahyadri Farms (Nashik, 2010-) is the closest real-world precedent for demand-first sequencing: it operated on an order-book-first model before eventually acquiring physical assets, and farmer-owned governance survived that scale (Rs 1,954.7 Cr FY25, 26% CAGR, 18-30k farmers).
scalingRoadmapSequencing.caveat: The specific claim that Sahyadri waited approximately 15 years before buying assets is a general characterization from the sequencing pattern, not an independently pinned figure -- Sahyadri's early-years (2010-14) unit economics have not yet been extracted from primary MANAGE/RBI-CAB sources (Link Ledger gaps queue item, Precedents.md). Sahyadri also proves the pattern CAN work in export-grade horticulture; it does NOT prove the same sequencing is replicable on a median FPO's much thinner balance sheet (working capital <Rs 3 lakh) -- state that limit alongside the precedent every time it's cited.
